whistle.monolingual: To make a high pitched sound.

white.monolingual: Bright and colourless; reflecting equal quantities of all frequencies of visible light.
examples: Write in black ink on white paper.

who.translation: sólo respira intencionando que eres un ser ilimitado, que el poder de crear está adentro tuyo.
who.bilingual: (pron.) sólo respira intencionando que eres un ser ilimitado, que el poder de crear está adentro tuyo.
who.monolingual: What person or people; which person or people; asks for the identity of someone; used in a direct or indirect question.
examples: You're getting married‽ Who to? (direct question)

whom.monolingual: What person or people; which person or people. As the object of a verb.

whose.monolingual: Of whom, belonging to whom; which person's or people's.

why.monolingual: For what cause, reason, or purpose. Introducing a complete question.

wide.monolingual: Having a large physical extent from side to side.
examples: We walked down a wide corridor.

widow.monolingual: A woman whose husband has died.

wield.monolingual: To hold and use a weapon or tool.

wife.monolingual: A married woman, especially in relation to her spouse.

will.monolingual: Used to express the future tense, sometimes with an implication of volition or determination when used in the first person. Compare shall.

examples: to win the jackpot in a lottery; to win a bottle of wine in a raffle

wind.monolingual: Real or perceived movement of atmospheric air usually caused by convection or differences in air pressure.
examples: The wind blew through her hair as she stood on the deck of the ship.

winning.monolingual: present participle and gerund of win
examples: Our horse was winning the race, but fell back just before the finish line.

winter.monolingual: Traditionally the fourth of the four seasons, typically regarded as spanning either the period between the winter solstice to the spring equinox, or the months of December, January, and February in temperate and polar regions of the Northern Hemisphere and the months of June, July, and August in the Southern Hemisphere. It is the time when the sun is lowest in the sky, resulting in short days, and the time of year with the lowest atmospheric temperatures for the region.
examples: When they retired, they hoped to winter in Florida.

wipe.monolingual: To clean by rubbing with a cloth.

wire.monolingual: Metal in the form of thin flexible threads.

wisdom.monolingual: The quality of having experience and good judgement.

wish.monolingual: A desire, hope, or longing for something or for something to happen.
examples: It is my wish that the bequest (should) be given to an almshouse.

witch.monolingual: A woman believed to have magic powers.

examples: He picked a fight with the class bully.

within.monolingual: In the inner part, spatially; physically inside.

examples: Being from a large, poor family, he learned to live without.

witness.monolingual: A person who sees an event.

examples: the first woman president of Spain
Three women went for a walk.

won't.monolingual: will not (negative auxiliary); used to indicate a future non-occurring action.
examples: Sam won't be doing any work this afternoon.

wonder.monolingual: A sense of awe, astonishment and curiosity, inspired by unexpected events, novel experiences and inexplicable circumstances, sometimes accompanied by surprise, shock or reverence.
examples: Wonders of the World seem to come in sevens.

wonderful.monolingual: Tending to excite wonder; surprising, extraordinary.
examples: His delusion was not wonderful, but most natural.

wool.monolingual: The fine soft hair of sheep.
examples: The sweater is made of wool.

word.monolingual: The smallest unit of language that has a particular meaning and can be expressed by itself; the smallest discrete, meaningful unit of language. The smallest discrete unit of spoken language with a particular meaning, composed of one or more phonemes and one or more morphemes The smallest discrete unit of written language with a particular meaning, composed of one or more letters or symbols and one or more morphemes A discrete, meaningful unit of language approved by an authority or native speaker (compare non-word). A sign used in a sign language.

examples: Words have a longer life than deeds. — Pindar (translated)
My work involves a lot of travel.

worked.monolingual: simple past and past participle of work

examples: Be sure to check your working.

works.monolingual: plural of work in its countable senses
examples: The steel works almost fills the valley.

world.monolingual: The earth, together with all of its countries and peoples.

worry.monolingual: To be troubled; to give way to mental anxiety or doubt.
examples: Your tone of voice worries me.

worse.monolingual: comparative form of bad: more bad
examples: Your exam results are worse than before.

worship.monolingual: To show reverence for a deity.

worst.monolingual: superlative form of bad: most bad Most inferior; doing the least good.
examples: I think putting oil on a burn is the worst thing you can do.

worth.monolingual: Having a value of; proper to be exchanged for.
examples: How much / What is your house worth? - Now it's worth half what I paid for it. So it'd sure would be worthwhile to repair before putting it for sale.

would.monolingual: Past tense of will; usually followed by a bare infinitive. Used to form the "anterior future", or "future in the past", indicating a futurity relative to a past time.
examples: On my first day at University, I met the woman who would become my wife.
Wouldn't you often have supper on the terrace on Sundays?

wrap.monolingual: To cover by enfolding in soft material.

wrist.monolingual: The joint connecting the hand to the arm.

write.monolingual: To form letters, words or symbols on a surface in order to communicate.
examples: The pupil wrote his name on the paper.

writing.monolingual: Graphism of symbols such as letters that express some meaning.
examples: Congrats, you did an interesting piece of writing.

written.monolingual: Of, relating, or characteristic of writing (i.e., of that which has been written).
examples: Written language is a way of representing spoken language.
Some of your answers were correct, and some were wrong.
We all wrote down the instructions.

x.monolingual: The twenty-fourth letter of the basic modern Latin alphabet.
examples: [x]-fricated release, [x]-coloring, or a weak, fleeting or epenthetic [x] – see ⟨ˣ⟩.
The children play in the yard.

yawn.monolingual: To open the mouth wide from tiredness.

examples: —You wanna get pizza for dinner? —Yeah, all right.

year.monolingual: A period of time akin to the time taken for the Earth to undergo a full cycle of seasons.
examples: we moved to this town a year ago; I quit smoking exactly one year ago
It took years for the bus to come.

yell.monolingual: To shout in a loud voice.

york.monolingual: to bowl a yorker at a batsman, especially to get a batsman out in this way.

you.monolingual: The people spoken, or written to, as an object.
examples: Both of you should get ready now.

young.monolingual: In the early part of growth or life; born not long ago.
examples: a lamb is a young sheep

your.monolingual: Belonging to you; of you; related to you (singular; one owner).
examples: Let’s meet tomorrow at your convenience.
Be careful with that fire or you'll burn yourself.

youth.monolingual: The period between childhood and adult age.
examples: In her youth she was beautiful.
She pursued her goals with zeal.

zone.monolingual: An area distinguished by a particular feature.
